questions.q: I'M SAFE checklist — what does each letter represent?
choices: Instruments Mixture Speed Altitude Fuel Engine
Illness Medication Stress Alcohol Fatigue Emotion — personal fitness self-assessment before flight
Ice Moisture Storms Airspace Fog Equipment
Ignition Magnetos Stall speed AOA Fuel ELT
questions.explanation: I'M SAFE: Illness (am I sick?), Medication (any impairing drugs?), Stress (psychological condition?), Alcohol (within 8-hour and 0.04% BAC limits?), Fatigue (adequately rested?), Emotion (emotionally stable?).

questions.q: Night vision begins to deteriorate from hypoxia above approximately what altitude?
choices: 3000 ft MSL
5000 ft MSL
10000 ft MSL
14000 ft MSL
questions.explanation: Night vision is the first faculty affected by hypoxia, beginning to deteriorate at approximately 5000 ft MSL. Supplemental oxygen is recommended for night flights above 5000 ft.

questions.q: Above what altitude must the pilot use supplemental oxygen continuously?
choices: Above 12500 ft MSL
Above 14000 ft MSL
Above 15000 ft MSL
Above 18000 ft MSL
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.211: Above 14000 ft MSL the pilot must use supplemental oxygen for the entire duration of flight.

questions.q: Passengers must be provided supplemental oxygen above what altitude?
choices: Above 12500 ft MSL
Above 14000 ft MSL
Above 15000 ft MSL
Above 18000 ft MSL
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.211(a)(3): Above 15000 ft MSL the PIC must provide supplemental oxygen to each occupant.

questions.q: Maximum blood alcohol content allowed when acting as a crewmember?
choices: 0.02 percent
0.04 percent
0.06 percent
0.08 percent
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.17(a)(4): Blood alcohol concentration must be less than 0.04 percent. Note this is lower than the legal driving limit in most states.

questions.q: Symptoms of hyperventilation and how is it treated?
choices: Identical to hypoxia but only occurs at high altitude; treat with descent
Lightheadedness, tingling fingers, dizziness, muscle spasms from excess CO2 loss; treat by consciously slowing breathing, talking aloud, or breathing into a bag
Treat immediately with supplemental oxygen
Symptoms only at altitude; no treatment
questions.explanation: Hyperventilation results from breathing too rapidly and exhausting CO2. Symptoms: tingling, dizziness, visual disturbances. Treatment: slow breathing consciously, talk aloud, or breathe into a bag. Check altitude first to differentiate from hypoxia.

questions.q: What is the leans illusion?
choices: The sensation of pitching up when actually in level flight
A false sensation of being in a bank after returning to straight-and-level flight — the pilot may unconsciously re-bank to restore the feel of level
A visual illusion caused by sloping terrain beneath the aircraft
Only occurs when an instrument fails
questions.explanation: The leans: gradual bank develops below the vestibular detection threshold. Upon returning to level the pilot senses the opposite bank and may unconsciously re-bank to feel level again.

questions.q: Carbon monoxide poisoning — symptoms and immediate action?
choices: Excessive oxygen — treat by reducing oxygen flow

Colorless odorless combustion byproduct entering cabin through heater leaks; symptoms: headache, drowsiness, gray vision; action: shut off heater, open fresh air vents, land immediately, use supplemental oxygen
Nitrogen toxicity requiring immediate descent
Only affects smokers with pre-existing lung conditions
questions.explanation: CO is colorless and odorless. Sources include heater exhaust leaks. Symptoms: headache, drowsiness, gray tint to vision, impaired judgment. Immediate action: shut off cabin heat, open fresh air vents, land as soon as possible, use supplemental oxygen.

questions.q: Visual illusion causing a pilot to fly an unexpectedly low approach at night over featureless terrain?
choices: Wide runway illusion causing the pilot to fly too high
Black hole approach — absence of visual cues causes pilots to fly a dangerously low approach; always use VASI or PAPI
Narrow runway illusion causing the pilot to fly too low
Upslope runway illusion making the runway appear longer
questions.explanation: Black hole approach illusion: landing toward unlighted terrain at night without surrounding visual cues. Pilots tend to fly an approach that appears visually correct but is actually dangerously low. Always use VASI, PAPI, or ILS.

questions.q: Upslope runway creates what visual illusion and what is the hazard?
choices: Makes aircraft appear lower than it is — pilot tends to fly a high approach
Makes aircraft appear higher than it is — pilot may fly a lower-than-normal approach and undershoot the runway
Has no effect on approach angle perception
Creates the same illusion as a downslope runway
questions.explanation: An upsloping runway appears shorter and creates the illusion that the aircraft is higher than normal. The pilot may respond by flying lower, actually undershooting. The opposite effect occurs with a downsloping runway.

questions.q: Euphoria is an early symptom of which condition?
choices: Hyperventilation — too much breathing
Hypoxia — early euphoria and false sense of well-being are insidious symptoms that may prevent the pilot from recognizing the condition
Carbon monoxide poisoning
Dehydration at altitude
questions.explanation: Hypoxia is insidious because early symptoms include euphoria and an exaggerated sense of well-being along with impaired judgment. The pilot may not realize anything is wrong until incapacitation.

questions.q: Pilot experiences tingling fingers and dizziness on approach at 6000 ft after a 2-hour flight — most likely cause?
choices: Hypoxia — 6000 ft is too high without supplemental oxygen
Hyperventilation from anxiety or stress — slow breathing consciously to diagnose and treat
Carbon monoxide poisoning from the heater system
Dehydration causing electrolyte imbalance
questions.explanation: At 6000 ft hypoxia is unlikely for healthy pilots (O2 required above 12500 ft). Tingling and dizziness during a potentially stressful approach phase is more consistent with hyperventilation. Slow the breathing rate to treat.

questions.q: What is the Coriolis illusion and when does it occur?
choices: A visual illusion from a sloping runway surface
Occurs when a pilot moves their head in a different plane while in a sustained turn — the inner ear senses rotation in multiple planes creating a strong tumbling or spinning sensation
The sensation of climbing when power is suddenly increased
A compass reading error in polar regions due to magnetic anomalies
questions.explanation: Coriolis illusion: when a pilot tilts their head in a different plane during a sustained turn, the semicircular canals receive conflicting signals causing a powerful and disorienting tumbling or spinning sensation. Avoid sudden head movements in IMC.

questions.q: Last drink at 8 PM. Earliest legal departure time as a crewmember?
choices: Midnight — 4 hours is sufficient
4:00 AM the following day — 8 hours after the last drink
10:00 PM — 2 hours is enough if you feel fine
Any time you personally feel capable of flying safely
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.17(a)(2): 8-hour bottle-to-throttle minimum. Last drink at 8 PM means earliest legal departure is 4:00 AM. The FAA recommends 24 hours from last drink for maximum safety.

questions.q: A pilot is on an OTC medication and feels no side effects. Can they legally and safely fly?
choices: Yes — no perceived side effects means the medication is safe for flight
No — many OTC medications have physiological effects on aviation performance even without noticeable symptoms; always consult an AME before flying on any medication
Yes — over-the-counter medications are always permitted for flight
Only prescription medications require AME consultation before flying
questions.explanation: Many OTC medications including antihistamines, decongestants, and some pain relievers can cause sedation, impaired judgment, or other effects without the pilot noticing. Always consult an Aviation Medical Examiner (AME) before flying on any medication.

questions.q: At what altitude between 12500 and 14000 ft MSL must the pilot use supplemental oxygen?
choices: Never — O2 only required above 14000
When this altitude range is flown for more than 30 minutes
Anytime above 12500 MSL regardless of duration
Only above 13500 MSL
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.211(a)(1): Between 12500 and 14000 ft MSL, the required flight crewmember must use supplemental oxygen for the portions of flight exceeding 30 minutes at that altitude band.

questions.q: What is spatial disorientation?
choices: Becoming geographically lost during a cross-country flight
A false sense of the aircraft's attitude, motion, or position caused by misleading sensory inputs — corrected by trusting and flying by flight instruments
A condition that only occurs when instruments fail
A gradual disorientation from fatigue over long flights
questions.explanation: Spatial disorientation occurs when the vestibular system and other senses provide misleading information about aircraft attitude or motion. In IMC, pilots must disregard sensory feelings and trust flight instruments.

questions.q: Narrow runway illusion — what approach error does it cause?
choices: Pilot flies a lower than normal approach — risk of undershooting
Pilot flies a higher than normal approach — risk of landing long or floating
No effect on approach angle perception
Same as wide runway illusion but less severe
questions.explanation: A narrow runway appears to be farther away, making the pilot think the aircraft is higher than normal. This leads to a lower approach and risk of undershooting or hitting obstacles short of the runway.

questions.q: What is the graveyard spin illusion?
choices: A visual illusion from spinning runway approach lighting

During a prolonged spin the body adapts to the rotation and the pilot feels they have stopped. When the spin actually stops, the pilot senses rotation in the opposite direction and may re-enter the spin.
A spin that develops during the landing flare
An optical illusion that makes the ground appear to spin during low-level flight
questions.explanation: During a prolonged spin, the semicircular canals adapt and stop sensing rotation. When the spin is stopped, the sudden change feels like rotation in the opposite direction and the pilot may re-enter the spin to stop the false sensation.

questions.q: What is hypoxia and what makes hypemic hypoxia particularly dangerous?
choices: Excess oxygen in the bloodstream — rare at altitude
Oxygen deficiency at the cellular level. Hypemic hypoxia (carbon monoxide poisoning) is considered particularly dangerous because CO has no color or smell and the pilot may feel fine initially.
Only occurs above 18000 ft MSL
Hypoxia only affects unfit or elderly pilots
questions.explanation: Hypoxia is oxygen deficiency at the cellular level. Types include hypoxic (altitude), hypemic (CO poisoning/anemia), stagnant (circulation failure), and histotoxic (alcohol/drugs). Hypemic hypoxia from CO is insidious because CO is colorless and odorless.

questions.q: A pilot notices their thinking is slowing down and they feel unusually warm and comfortable at FL100 in IMC. What should they suspect?
choices: Normal physiological response to cruise flight — nothing to worry about
Possible hypoxia — descend to lower altitude immediately, use supplemental oxygen, and reassess the situation
Hyperventilation — increase breathing rate to restore alertness
Dehydration — drink water and continue the flight
questions.explanation: Slowed thinking, warmth, and unusual comfort at altitude are classic hypoxia symptoms. The insidious nature means the pilot may not recognize the impairment. Immediate action: descend, use oxygen, and assess whether flight can safely continue.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the I'M SAFE checklist?
choices: To check the aircraft before flight — same as TOMATO FLAMES
To provide a personal self-assessment of pilot fitness to fly before each flight, covering the most common human factors that cause accidents
To check ATC frequencies before flight
To verify weather is above VFR minimums
questions.explanation: I'M SAFE is a personal minimums and human factors checklist. A pilot who identifies a concern in any category — illness, impairing medication, excessive stress, alcohol within limits, fatigue, or emotional distress — should not fly.

questions.q: Continuing to fly while knowingly ill, on impairing medication, or while significantly fatigued — what regulatory concept applies?
choices: The pilot must self-certify fitness using the I'M SAFE checklist and may not legally fly if any item is a concern
Only a medical doctor can ground a pilot
FAA will notify the pilot if they are unfit to fly
Self-certification is not required — it is always the airline's responsibility
questions.explanation: Pilots are responsible for self-certifying their fitness to fly. The I'M SAFE checklist formalizes this. A pilot who is ill, on impairing medication, excessively fatigued, or emotionally distressed should not exercise pilot privileges.

questions.q: What does histotoxic hypoxia mean?
choices: Hypoxia caused by altitude above 10000 ft
Hypoxia where the cells cannot use oxygen properly due to a toxic substance such as alcohol or drugs even when oxygen levels are adequate
Hypoxia caused by low blood circulation
Hypoxia caused by carbon monoxide binding to hemoglobin
questions.explanation: Histotoxic hypoxia: cells cannot properly utilize oxygen due to toxic substances like alcohol, narcotics, or certain drugs, even when oxygen partial pressure is adequate. This explains why alcohol significantly worsens altitude effects.

questions.q: What are the two factors that make hypoxia particularly dangerous in aviation?
choices: It is very painful and occurs suddenly
Symptoms can be insidious with the pilot feeling well and even euphoric, and judgment is impaired before the pilot recognizes the problem
Hypoxia only affects vision so the pilot can still fly instruments
Hypoxia is easily recognizable by all pilots with proper training
questions.explanation: Hypoxia is dangerous because: (1) early symptoms include euphoria and a false sense of well-being (insidious onset), and (2) judgment is impaired before the pilot recognizes the problem, preventing appropriate self-corrective action.

questions.q: What is flicker vertigo and when does it occur?
choices: A type of vertigo caused only by turbulence

Flicker vertigo can occur when sunlight flickers through rotating propeller blades at certain frequencies (4 to 20 cycles per second) — can cause nausea, dizziness, unconsciousness, or even seizures in susceptible individuals. Wearing sunglasses reduces risk.
Flicker vertigo only affects helicopter pilots
Flicker vertigo is a myth — not recognized medically
questions.explanation: Flicker vertigo (photic driving or photosensitive epilepsy) can be induced when light flickers in the frequency range of 4 to 20 cycles per second — which can occur with a propeller blocking sunlight. Symptoms range from nausea and dizziness to loss of consciousness. Wearing sunglasses reduces the flicker effect.

questions.q: What is the somatogravic illusion and when is it most dangerous?
choices: An illusion only experienced by astronauts

The somatogravic illusion occurs during rapid acceleration — the pilot senses a nose-high pitch attitude (like a gravity vector pulling them back into the seat) when actually accelerating in level flight. Most dangerous during a go-around or catapult launch — can cause the pilot to push forward into the ground.
The somatogravic illusion only occurs at night
The somatogravic illusion is the same as the leans
questions.explanation: The somatogravic illusion: during rapid acceleration, the increased G-force causes the pilot to sense a nose-high pitch attitude (false climb sensation). This can cause the pilot to push forward on the controls, pitching the nose down at low altitude. Most dangerous during go-around, takeoff, or cat shots — especially at night or in IMC.

questions.q: What is time of useful consciousness (TUC) and why is it important?
choices: TUC is the time before a pilot must land to avoid fatigue

TUC is the time after oxygen supply is lost during which a pilot retains the ability to perform useful mental and physical tasks before hypoxia causes incapacitation. TUC decreases rapidly with increasing altitude — at FL250 it may be only 3-5 minutes, at FL350 it may be 30-60 seconds.
TUC is the maximum flight time per day
TUC is the time between engine starts at high altitude airports
questions.explanation: Time of Useful Consciousness (TUC) is the time after oxygen supply is interrupted (or hypoxia begins) during which the pilot can still perform purposeful actions. At FL180: ~20-30 min. At FL250: ~3-5 min. At FL350: ~30-60 sec. At FL430: ~9-12 sec. This is why rapid donning of oxygen equipment is critical at high altitude.

questions.q: What is motion sickness and how should a pilot manage it?
choices: Motion sickness has no effect on flight safety because it is only gastrointestinal

Motion sickness causes nausea, vomiting, sweating, disorientation, and fatigue — significantly impairing pilot performance. Management: focus on horizon or fixed points, avoid reading, use fresh air/ventilation, and in severe cases, land. OTC anti-motion sickness drugs are generally disqualifying.
Motion sickness is normal and does not affect flight performance
Motion sickness only occurs in turbulence and resolves when turbulence ends
questions.explanation: Motion sickness significantly impairs pilot performance through nausea, dizziness, fatigue, and disorientation. In early stages: focus on outside horizon, control ventilation, avoid tasks requiring head movements. Most OTC remedies (Dramamine, Benadryl) cause sedation and are disqualifying. Severe motion sickness is an I'M SAFE concern.

questions.q: What effect does high altitude flight have on trapped gas in body cavities?
choices: High altitude reduces trapped gas — beneficial for body cavities

Boyle's Law: as pressure decreases with altitude, trapped gas expands. Gas in the middle ear, sinuses, gastrointestinal tract, and any trapped gas from dental work or recent surgery can cause significant pain. Flying with a cold or upper respiratory infection can trap gas in the middle ear causing aerotitis media.
High altitude has no effect on body gas — only affects air in tanks
This only applies to unpressurized aircraft above 25,000 ft
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: gas volume is inversely proportional to pressure. As altitude increases (lower pressure), trapped gas in body cavities expands. Middle ear and sinus problems (barotitis/barosinusitis) from trapped gas are serious aeromedical hazards. Never fly with a cold that blocks sinus or Eustachian tube drainage. This affects ALL altitudes in unpressurized aircraft.

questions.q: What is the sclerae hypoxia effect and at what altitude does night vision first degrade?
choices: Sclerae hypoxia causes the whites of the eyes to turn blue at altitude

Night vision begins to degrade at approximately 5000 ft MSL because the rods in the retina (which detect low light) are extremely sensitive to hypoxia. Supplemental oxygen at night is recommended even at altitudes well below legal requirements.
Night vision degrades only above 14000 ft
Night vision is not affected by altitude
questions.explanation: The rod cells in the retina, which provide night (low-light) vision, are extremely sensitive to hypoxia. Night visual acuity begins to degrade at approximately 5000 ft MSL — significantly below the altitude at which supplemental oxygen is legally required (12,500 ft). Use of supplemental oxygen on night flights is strongly recommended above 5000 ft.

questions.q: What is the recommended off-axis scanning technique for night vision?
choices: Look directly at objects for best night vision

Look approximately 5-10 degrees off to the side of an object rather than directly at it — the center of the retina (fovea) contains cones (color/detail vision) which are less sensitive in low light. The peripheral retina has more rods which are better for low-light detection.
Night vision is best when looking directly into bright lights
Focus directly at runway threshold lights for best night vision
questions.explanation: The fovea (center of the retina) contains cones which detect color and detail but require more light. In low-light conditions, rods in the peripheral retina provide better detection. By looking approximately 5-10 degrees off to the side of an object (off-axis viewing), you use the more sensitive rods rather than the less-sensitive fovea.

questions.q: What are the symptoms of dehydration at altitude and how does it affect pilot performance?
choices: Dehydration has no measurable effect on pilot performance

Dehydration at altitude causes fatigue, reduced concentration, headache, and impaired decision-making — often misidentified as other conditions. The low humidity in aircraft cabins accelerates dehydration. Pilots should drink water regularly during flight.
Dehydration only affects physical performance, not cognitive performance
Dehydration effects are cancelled by the pressurization system
questions.explanation: Dehydration impairs cognitive performance and contributes to fatigue, poor decision-making, and susceptibility to hypoxia effects. Low humidity in aircraft cabins (especially at altitude) accelerates fluid loss even without obvious sweating. Pilots should drink water regularly before and during flight and avoid caffeine and alcohol which worsen dehydration.

questions.q: What is the danger of self-medicating with over-the-counter pain relievers before flight?
choices: OTC pain relievers are always safe for flight

Many OTC pain relievers can impair judgment, cause drowsiness (antihistamines in combination products), mask pain indicating a serious underlying condition, or have drug interactions. Aspirin can affect platelets; ibuprofen and naproxen can cause gastrointestinal issues at altitude.
OTC pain relievers only affect pilots on long flights
Pain relievers have no effect on pilot cognitive performance
questions.explanation: OTC medications require careful consideration before flight. Combination products (cold and flu medications) often contain antihistamines causing drowsiness. Aspirin affects platelet function. Any medication that treats a condition (pain, congestion) may mask symptoms of a medical condition that itself is disqualifying. Always consult an AME before flying on any medication.

questions.q: What is the danger of flying within 24 hours of scuba diving?
choices: No danger — scuba diving and flying are compatible activities

After scuba diving, dissolved nitrogen in the body tissues can form bubbles (decompression sickness / the bends) when exposed to lower pressure at altitude. The FAA recommends waiting at least 12 hours after a no-decompression dive and at least 24 hours after decompression diving or multiple dives.
The danger is only for dives below 100 feet
The danger only applies if the pilot had ear problems during diving
questions.explanation: Scuba diving dissolves nitrogen into body tissues under pressure. At altitude, lower pressure can cause dissolved nitrogen to form bubbles in tissues and blood (decompression sickness), causing joint pain, paralysis, or death. FAA guidelines: wait at least 12 hours after a single no-decompression dive; at least 24 hours after multiple dives, decompression dives, or any problems during diving.

questions.q: What is the 'DECIDE' model in aeronautical decision-making?
choices: A checklist for engine failure procedures
Detect a problem, Estimate need to react, Choose a course of action, Identify actions needed, Do the necessary actions, Evaluate the effect — a structured in-flight decision-making process
A weather evaluation system
An ATC communication protocol
questions.explanation: DECIDE: Detect (recognize a change), Estimate (assess need to react), Choose (select best action), Identify (determine specific steps), Do (implement), Evaluate (assess outcome and repeat). Provides a systematic approach to in-flight decision-making under pressure.

questions.q: What are the five hazardous attitudes identified by the FAA?
choices: Overconfidence, carelessness, laziness, haste, and fear
Anti-authority, Impulsivity, Invulnerability, Macho, and Resignation — each has a specific antidote
Speed, ego, pride, stubbornness, peer pressure
Risk, reward, urgency, habit, tradition
questions.explanation: Five hazardous attitudes and antidotes: Anti-authority ('Follow the rules') Impulsivity ('Not so fast, think first') Invulnerability ('It could happen to me') Macho ('Taking chances is foolish') Resignation ('I'm not helpless'). Recognizing and correcting these attitudes is a key ADM skill.

questions.q: What is the impact of prescription medications on flight safety?
choices: All prescription medications are approved for flight if the pilot feels fine

Many prescription medications have side effects (sedation, impaired judgment, reduced reaction time) that can be dangerous in flight even without obvious subjective effects; always consult an AME before flying on any medication
Prescription medications are safer than OTC medications for flight
Only controlled substances require AME consultation
questions.explanation: Prescription medications may cause side effects that impair pilot performance, be prescribed for conditions that are themselves disqualifying, or interact with altitude, stress, or other substances in unexpected ways. Never fly on prescription medication without explicit AME approval.

questions.q: Why should pilots use off-center (eccentric) viewing at night?
choices: To avoid glare from runway lights by looking to the side
The center of the retina (fovea) contains cones which require more light — looking 5-10 degrees off to the side uses the rod-rich peripheral retina which is more sensitive to low light levels
Night vision is equally good regardless of where on the retina the image falls
Off-center viewing is for reading charts only, not for spotting aircraft or obstacles
questions.explanation: The fovea (center of the retina) contains only cones, which detect color and fine detail but require relatively bright light. In darkness, rods in the peripheral retina provide much better detection ability. By looking 5-10 degrees off an object, the image falls on the rod-rich peripheral retina, dramatically improving detection of dim objects at night.

questions.q: How long does full dark adaptation take and what destroys it instantly?
choices: 5 minutes and is not affected by brief light exposure
Approximately 30 minutes for full dark adaptation — destroyed instantly by exposure to bright white light. Use red lighting in the cockpit to preserve dark adaptation.
Approximately 5 minutes and is only partially reduced by light exposure
Dark adaptation is permanent once achieved and is not affected by light exposure
questions.explanation: Full dark adaptation takes approximately 30 minutes and relies on a chemical process in the rod cells (rhodopsin builds up in darkness). A single flash of bright white light resets this process, destroying adaptation. Red light has minimal effect on rhodopsin and is used for cockpit lighting and chart reading to preserve night vision.

questions.q: Night currency to carry passengers requires what recent experience?
choices: 3 touch-and-goes at any time of day within 90 days
3 full-stop takeoffs and landings between 1 hour after sunset and 1 hour before sunrise within the preceding 90 days
Any 3 landings at night within the preceding 30 days
5 takeoffs and landings at night within the preceding 60 days
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.107(a)(3): To carry passengers at night, a pilot must have made 3 takeoffs and 3 full-stop landings during the period from 1 hour after sunset to 1 hour before sunrise within the preceding 90 days. Touch-and-goes do NOT satisfy the night currency requirement — full stops required.
